response: The global food system faces unprecedented strain as the world population is set to reach 9.7 billion by 2050, requiring a 60 percent increase in production amid climate-driven yield declines. Agriculture itself generates about 25 percent of greenhouse gas emissions and is hampered by water scarcity—exemplified by the over-drawn Ogallala Aquifer—and soil degradation that has cut productivity on one-third of the world’s arable land. Roughly one-third of all food is lost or wasted between farm and fork, further deepening the crisis. Proposed solutions range from AI-driven precision farming, vertical urban agriculture, alternative proteins, and drought-resistant crops to regenerative practices and policy reforms on subsidies, trade, and carbon pricing—but scaling these measures affordably and equitably remains the central challenge.
